planting.note: Plant divisions or rhizomes after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Lime Zinger is a compact xanthosoma reaching 2–3 feet (smaller than most elephant ears), making it well-suited for containers and small garden spaces. In zone 9 it grows vigorously in summer heat and humidity, reaching full size by midsummer. Unlike Colocasia, Xanthosoma does not need as much moisture and tolerates slightly drier conditions. In zone 9 the rhizomes can often overwinter in the ground with heavy mulching but are safest dug and stored indoors in frost-free conditions.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil; Lime Zinger is a compact Xanthosoma cultivar with exceptionally vivid, clear chartreuse to lime-yellow-green leaves that glow with an almost electric intensity in shaded garden settings. While most elephant ears are dark green or dramatically colored with dark burgundy or black, Lime Zinger's brilliant chartreuse-yellow foliage brings an entirely different quality of light and energy to shade gardens — the luminous yellow-green leaves reflect and amplify ambient light in ways that dark-foliaged plants cannot, visually brightening shaded areas that typically appear gloomy and low-energy. The compact 2–3 foot height distinguishes Lime Zinger from the 4–6 foot stature of larger elephant ear varieties, making it practical for smaller spaces, container cultivation, and foreground positions in shade garden designs. In zone 9's humid subtropical climate, Lime Zinger thrives from late spring through fall, rapidly producing new leaves that emerge chartreuse and mature to lime green with each unfurling adding drama to the seasonal garden progression.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Xanthosoma as Landscape Plants
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Aroids for Gulf Coast Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Elephant Ears for Gulf Coast Landscapes

planting.note: Plant divisions or corms after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Mammoth grows rapidly in zone 9's warm, humid summer conditions — plants can reach full height and enormous leaf size by midsummer from a spring planting. Provide consistent moisture, especially during peak summer growth; Colocasia thrives in wet to consistently moist conditions and can even grow in shallow water. In zone 9 the corms often survive winter in the ground with heavy mulching; in colder areas of zone 9 dig and store corms above 50°F. The enormous leaves can reach 4 feet across — a single plant in optimal conditions creates a truly dramatic, tropical focal point.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Mammoth Colocasia thrives in consistently moist conditions, bog edges, and even shallow water where drainage is poor; Mammoth is a large-growing selection of common taro (Colocasia esculenta) selected and named for its exceptional leaf size — producing the largest leaves of any widely available Colocasia cultivar, with individual leaves reaching 3–4 feet long and nearly as wide under optimal conditions. The enormous, heart-shaped, velvety, medium-to-dark green leaves on sturdy, upright petioles create an overwhelming tropical presence in the garden that is unmatched by almost any other hardy temperate-climate plant — a single well-grown Mammoth plant is a landscape focal point that demands attention and creates an immediate tropical atmosphere in whatever garden space it occupies. The enormous leaf surface is simultaneously visually dramatic and ecologically functional: Mammoth creates deep shade beneath its canopy (useful for understory shade plants), captures rainfall and funnels it to the soil (an adaptation to tropical forest conditions), and provides cover for garden wildlife. In zone 9 Mammoth thrives in the humid heat that is its native climate — producing increasingly large leaves through summer as the plant approaches its full potential.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Colocasia Species and Cultivars
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Elephant Ears for Gulf South Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Large Tropical Foliage Plants for Zone 9

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 move outdoors to a shaded location March–May. Rex begonias perform best with high humidity and consistent warmth, making zone 9's humid summer climate ideal for outdoor summer use in deep shade. Keep consistently moist but not waterlogged; water at soil level, not on leaves. Bring indoors when temperatures drop below 55°F. The large, colorful leaves are at their most vivid in spring and fall — summer heat and dry conditions may slightly mute the color intensity.

directions: Plant in deep shade to bright indirect light in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil — Rex begonias require consistent moisture and high humidity while being extremely sensitive to waterlogged soil; Fireworks is a large-leafed Rex begonia cultivar with a dramatically colorful, multi-zone leaf pattern that has become one of the most popular Rex begonia cultivars for its spectacular foliage display. The very large, asymmetrical leaves (12+ inches across) display multiple distinct color zones: the central area is deep silver-purple to lavender, surrounded by a contrasting zone of deep burgundy-red, then another ring of metallic silver, and finally dark green margins — creating a concentric ring pattern of contrasting colors that genuinely resembles the radial burst of a firework when viewed from above. The Rex begonia group (Begonia rex-cultorum) represents one of the most extraordinary examples of deliberate ornamental foliage development in horticulture — the plant is grown exclusively for its extraordinary leaf appearance, with the flowers considered insignificant compared to the foliage. Fireworks pushes the Rex begonia form to an extreme of color variety and leaf size that is genuinely difficult to believe in a living plant, making it one of the most conversation-provoking houseplants or shade container plants available.
sources: American Begonia Society — Rex Begonia Species and Cultivar List
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rex Begonias for Shade and Interior
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Foliage Plants for Shade Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow after all frost danger has passed when soil is thoroughly warm (70°F+); in zone 9 plant April–July. Yard-long beans are a twining vine requiring a trellis, fence, or support of at least 6 feet tall — they are not self-supporting and will sprawl without structure. Harvest pods when 14–18 inches long and still tender and pliable; longer pods become tough and stringy. Harvest every 2–3 days during peak production — pods develop very rapidly in hot weather. In zone 9 these beans thrive in the summer heat that stresses or stops production of common snap beans.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with sturdy trellis support; Asparagus Yard-Long Bean is a traditional variety of the Chinese long bean (Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis), producing impressively long (18–24 inch), slender, pale green to light-colored pods that hang in pairs from the vines, resembling bundles of very long, thin asparagus spears in their pendant arrangement. The flavor of Asparagus yard-long beans is distinctly different from common snap beans — denser, less watery, slightly nutty, with a pleasant asparagus-like quality that explains the variety name. The texture holds up well to stir-frying and high-heat cooking that would make common snap beans too soft, making yard-long beans the preferred bean for stir-fries, curries, and Asian vegetable preparations where snap beans would become unpleasantly mushy. As a heat-adapted legume related to black-eyed peas rather than to common beans (Phaseolus), yard-long beans genuinely thrive in zone 9's summer conditions — producing abundantly in temperatures that stop snap bean production — making them one of the most practical fresh-bean crops for the Gulf South summer garden.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Yard-Long Beans for Florida Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Asian Vegetables for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Warm-Season Vegetables for Texas

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed when soil is thoroughly warm; in zone 9 plant April–June. Green Malabar Spinach produces entirely green stems and foliage, lacking the ornamental purple-red stem color of the red form but otherwise identical in culture and culinary use. Provide a trellis, fence, or string support — the vines reach 6–10 feet in a full season. Harvest regularly by cutting stem tips 4–6 inches long to stimulate branching; regular harvesting keeps production high throughout the season. Soak seeds overnight before planting to improve germination, which can be slow and irregular.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; Green Malabar Spinach is the all-green form of Basella alba, producing the same climbing vine, thick succulent leaves, and heat-tolerant growth habit as the red-stemmed form but with completely green stems, petioles, and developing berries. The green form is less ornamental than the red-stemmed type but produces the same culinary product — thick, glossy, succulent leaves with a mild, slightly mucilaginous flavor that makes an excellent heat-season substitute for true spinach (Spinacia oleracea) during zone 9's summers when cool-season spinach is impossible to grow. The green form's less distinctive appearance makes it blend more naturally into vegetable garden beds without the ornamental statement of the red-stemmed variety — a practical choice for gardeners who want the culinary benefit without the color drama. The slight mucilage in the leaves and stems — the same compound that gives okra its characteristic texture — naturally thickens soups, stews, and green smoothies and contributes a smooth, slightly viscous mouthfeel to cooked preparations. In zone 9's summer heat and humidity, Malabar spinach (both forms) is one of the most productive leafy green crops available.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Malabar Spinach for Florida Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Greens for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Warm-Season Leafy Vegetables
